observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to (i) avail of the one-time cleansing and initiate a request for write off of all dormant accounts covered by COA Circular No. 2023-008; and (ii) impose appropriate actions to enforce the collection of the Other Receivables account totaling ₱5,563,096.31. Moreover, we recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant ensure that the receivables reflected in the financial statements are consistently monitored, verified and validated to determine the existence of dormant receivable accounts.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Municipality sent demand letters to accountable officers who are still living; however only ₱3,000.00 has been collected. The Municipality has not yet submitted a request for write-off due to difficulties in obtaining certain supporting documents such as PSA death certificates and other proofs. The OIC-Municipal Accountant will prepare the appropriate adjusting entry to record the net loss associated with unrecovered savings/time deposits from the former RBIC.
observations.reason: Due to difficulties encountered in securing the supporting documents, the Municipality has not yet submitted any request for write off for dormant receivables, including those of deceased accountable officers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to cancel the stale
checks amounting to ₱626,487.82, revert the same to the payable account,
and ensure compliance with procedural guidelines on the cancellation and
replacement of stale checks prescribed in the Manual on the NGAS for
LGUs, Volume I to arrive at the accurate cash in bank balance per books;

b. Municipal Accountant to (i) record the book reconciling items amounting
to ₱10,180.00, and submit the journal entry voucher (JEV) together with
the supporting documents to the Audit Team; (ii) cause the prompt
preparation and timely submission of BRS for all bank accounts duly
supported with bank statements; and (iii) furnish the MTO with the copies
of the BRS; and

c. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all collections are remitted intact and
regularly pursuant to Section 32 of the Cash Examination Manual.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Municipal Mayor (i) avail of
the one-time cleansing and initiate a request for write off of all dormant accounts
covered by COA Circular No. 2023-008; and (ii) impose appropriate actions to
enforce the collection of receivables recorded under the Other Receivables account
totaling ₱5,560,096.31. Moreover, we recommended that the OIC-Municipal
Accountant ensure that the receivables reflected in the financial statements are
consistently monitored, verified and validated to determine the existence of
dormant receivable accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete the physical count of all
inventory items; and (ii) prepare the required RPCI for all inventory
accounts, and submit to the Audit Team not later than July 31 and January
31 of each year, for the first and second semesters, respectively;

b. Property Custodian to maintain SCs for all inventories for proper
monitoring of receipt, issuance and accountability of inventory items;

c. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain SLCs for each inventory
item to properly track inventory movements; and (ii) prepare adjusting
entry to correct the recorded undelivered inventory items and ensure
adherence to Sections 120 and 122 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs,
Volume I in the proper recognition of inventories in the books of accounts;
and

d. Municipal Accountant and Property Custodian to reconcile the balances of
the inventory per books and inventory reports; and (ii) verify and analyze
the discrepancies, and prepare necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) require the concerned sellers of acquired lands to settle the Capital
Gains Tax and secure the latest Tax Declaration, in order to facilitate the transfer of ownership of the lands in the name
of the Municipal Government of Cajidiocan; and (ii) enhance property management, preventive maintenance planning,
and end-user accountability to maximize the full benefits from the continued and proper use of Municipality’s assets.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

a. Inventory Committee to (i) conduct a complete physical count of all PPE in accordance with COA Circular No.
2020-006, and upon completion, prepare the RPCPPE and submit the same to the Audit Team within the prescribed
period; and (ii) facilitate the immediate disposal of unserviceable properties and be guided of applicable provisions
on disposal of government properties;

b. Property Custodian to (i) prepare and maintain the Property Cards for each type of PPE; (ii) coordinate with the
Accounting Office for the reconciliation of their respective reports and make the necessary adjustments on any
discrepancies noted; (iii) prepare the IIRUP for unserviceable and demolished PPE items and furnish the same to the
MAO and the Audit Team; and (iv) submit a satisfactory explanation for the failure to submit a request for inspection
from COA prior to the demolition of PPE items; and

c. Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare and maintain complete property records such as PPE Ledger Card for each type
of PPE; (ii) prepare necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the semi-expendable properties costing below the
₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold to appropriate accounts; and (iii) properly disclose in the Notes to FS the demolished
PPEs totaling ₱1,782,746.68.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) make a representation with
the DOH for the applicable guidelines regarding the PhilHealth Capitation Fund;
(ii) implement the outstanding fund transfer for its intended purpose and/or
communicate with the respective source agencies to use the unutilized funds for
other programs of the Municipality in line with the same guidelines provided by the
latter. Otherwise, return the unutilized funds to their respective agencies so they
can use it to support other programs/campaigns of the government.

We also recommended that the OIC –Municipal Accountant: (i) cause the
refund of the unutilized funds to their source agencies after the completion of
project and/or upon the intended purpose has been served; (ii) exhaust all remedies
to collect the livelihood assistance given by the DSWD to Typhoon Frank victims
and remit the amount collected, if there’s any. Otherwise, request for write-off in
accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and (iii)
submit to the Audit Team the lacking FURs together with the copies of
vouchers/payrolls, and ensure that all liquidations are provided with liquidation
reports and proof of disbursement to prevent suspension in audit.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to enjoin the Municipal Treasurer and the OIC – Municipal Accountant to cancel the stale check amounting to ₱6,000.00, revert the same to the payable account, and make sure to observe the procedural guidelines on the cancellation and replacement of stale checks prescribed in the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I to arrive at the accurate cash in bank balance per books.
Moreover, the OIC – Municipal Accountant (i) ensure to adjust all reconciling items at year-end to reflect the correct amount of CIB - LCCA account in the FS; and (ii) cause the prompt preparation and timely submission of BRS for all bank accounts and furnish the MTO with a copy of monthly BRS together with the bank statements.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Municipal Accountant reverted the stale check amounting to ₱6,000.00 to payable accounts under JEV No. 100202402-386. The noted book reconciling items were already recorded under JEV Nos. 100202402-346, 100202406-1270, 100202406-1270(B). The noted unsubmitted BRS for CY 2023 were submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (i) implement the long outstanding fund transfers for its intended purpose and/or communicate with the respective source agencies to use the unutilized funds for other programs of the Municipality in line with the same guidelines provided by the latter. Otherwise, return the unutilized funds to their respective agencies. We also recommended that the OIC –Municipal Accountant (i) cause the refund of the unutilized funds to their source agencies after the completion of project and/or upon the intended purpose has been served; (ii) exhaust all remedies to collect the livelihood assistance given by the DSWD to Typhoon Frank victims and remit the amount collected, if there’s any. Otherwise, request for write-off in accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and (iii) submit to the Audit Team the lacking FURs together with the copies of vouchers/payrolls, and ensure that all liquidations are provided with liquidation reports and proof of disbursement to prevent suspension in audit.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Municipality refunded ₱1,742,400.00 to DSWD-Field Office MIMAROPA evidenced by Check No. 0094434-401 dated June 21, 2024. The Municipality obtained approval from NAPC to use unexpended balances to implement other programs. The balance of PhilHealth Capitation Fund ₱1,186,132.47 was proposed to be removed from Due to NGAs and treated as subsidy from the national government given authorization to exercise full discretion for its utilization. The Municipality will continue to make representation with the source agency for the remaining balance of ₱445,590.00 from livelihood assistance for typhoon “Frank” victims and refund unutilized amount of ₱850,086 to the Office of the President. The Municipal Accountant committed to submit the lacking FURs for the fund utilization totaling ₱5,910,769.88.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the: a. Inventory Committee to (i) properly plan and conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and local road networks and ensure the submission of the RPCPPE and Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System to the Office of the Auditor; and (ii) report all the unserviceable properties in the IIRUP supported with proper documentation for subsequent disposal; b. Municipal Accountant to (i) provide an allowance for depreciation for the ICT Equipment totaling ₱2,666,833.16 pursuant to IPSAS No. 17; (ii) prepare necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the misclassified PPE from the Furniture and Fixture Account to Other Machinery and Equipment account; and (iii) prepare and maintain complete property records such as PPE Ledger Card for each type of PPE; and (iv) prepare necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the semi-expendable properties costing below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold to appropriate accounts; and (v) properly disclose in the Notes to FS the demolished PPEs totaling ₱1,782,746.68; c. Property Custodian to (i) prepare the RPCPPE based on the physical count of the PPE and reconcile it with the Property Cards (PC); and (ii) coordinate with the Accounting Office for the reconciliation of their respective reports and make the necessary adjustments on any discrepancies noted.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The Municipal Accountant prepared JEV No. 100202403-575(L) for allowance of depreciation for ICT Equipment and JEV Nos. 100202403-575(L) and 100202403-575(M) for reclassification. The Physical Count of PPE conducted was incomplete; some procured items such as sports equipment were not included in the submitted RPCPPE. The IIRUP was prepared for the unserviceable items and demolished items were derecognized in the books under JEV No.100202505-965(D). Titles transfer of lands is underway. Plans for creation of a General Services Officer position were mentioned. Reclassification of semi-expendable properties costing below ₱50,000.00 were implemented under JEV No. 100202505-965(A).
observations.reason: The physical count was incomplete and some property records (Property Cards, PPELCs) were not fully prepared or maintained; reconciliation between Inventory Committee and MAO was not fully effected.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a) BAC and its Secretariat and Technical Working Group (TWG) to: (i) explain the noted deficiencies and lapses in the conduct of procurement and immediately submit all the lacking documents to the Municipal Accountant, for submission to the Audit Team; (ii) comply with the posting requirements for the NOA, Contract and NTP pursuant to Section 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the same Revised IRR; (iii) ensure that the contracts are perfected within the prescribed period and the submission of the same to the Audit Team within five days from its perfection; and (iv) for succeeding periods use the 6th Edition of PBD pursuant to GPPB Resolution No. 16-2020; and b) Municipal Accountant to immediately submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents for verification, and ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete documents, as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The BAC, Secretariat and TWG submitted written justifications and provided lacking documents; the agency stated delays were due to geographic location and remote coordination. The Audit Team acknowledged receipt of documents. The agency adopted the 6th Edition of PBDs for procurements carried out in late CY 2024. The Municipal Accountant submitted lacking documents for verification.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC and its Secretariat and TWG immediately submit all the lacking bidding documents to the Municipal Accountant, for submission to the Audit Team; and be cautious during the bid evaluation and post-qualification phase, for proper evaluation and assessment of the legal, technical and financial requirements of the prospective bidders. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents for verification, and ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete documents, as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001; impose liquidated damages totaling ₱1,660.94; and ensure that delivery of all projects are strictly monitored so that corresponding liquidated damages are imposed in the event that suppliers fail to satisfactorily deliver the goods or services within the specified delivery schedule.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The BAC/TWG submitted lacking documents; the Municipal Accountant submitted the noted lacking documents for verification. Liquidated damages amounting to ₱1,660.94 were deducted from the claim of the concerned contractor under DV No. 100202411-2320 dated November 20, 2024. The Municipal Accountant and BAC committed to strictly monitor deliveries and to enforce liquidated damages when applicable.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to: (i) confirm the feasibility of the identified priority development projects under the 20 percent DF; and (ii) ensure that the development projects to be funded out of the 20 percent DF are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; and (b) direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) fast track the implementation of the identified development PPAs at earlier dates to complete the projects within the year and maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF; and (ii) ensure that issues causing the non-implementation of project are reported immediately to address the same.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The MPDC ensured PPAs to be funded by the 20% DF are planned and procurement-and-implementation ready. The Municipal Engineer monitored the implementation and the Municipality achieved a high utilization rate of 85.69 percent for the 20% DF. Some projects were realigned when infeasible (e.g., issues concerning lot usage).

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to instruct the MDRRMO to (i) ensure that all PPAs to be funded out of the LDRRMF are sufficiently funded and viable to be implemented within its target of implementation; and (ii) identify disaster-related PPAs to be funded out of the LDRRMF unexpended balances from prior years and ensure to incorporate the identified PPAs in the annual LDRRMFIP. Henceforth, ensure timely implementation of disaster-related PPAs to guarantee that the objectives of mitigating the impact of disaster and enhancing disaster preparedness and response capabilities will be achieved.
observations.managementAction: Implemented In CY 2024, out of 38 planned disaster-related PPAs, only 19 PPAs were completed, three PPAs were partially completed and 16 were not implemented. The MDRRMO and Municipal Accountant committed to reconcile records and incorporate prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF projects into the LDRRMFIP; reconciliation and further implementation monitoring are ongoing.
observations.reason: Some PPAs were not implemented due to late procurement, lack of feasible sites (e.g., resettlement lots), duplication of assistance from other agencies, supplier unavailability, and other operational constraints; reconciliation of records between MDRRMO and MAO remained pending.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to ensure that all GAD-mandated PPAs are provided with adequate resources to warrant its implementation. Also, the Municipal Mayor agreed to, through the GAD Focal Point System: (i) prepare the Municipality’s GAD Agenda pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as basis in the formulation of the annual GPB; (ii) see to it that the GPB is prepared and submitted promptly to the oversight bodies for review and obtain its subsequent endorsement within the prescribed period; (iii) coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records to achieve uniformity in the presentation of utilization of GAD Fund in their respective reports; (iv) enjoin the GFPS to observe timely submission of the GPB and its HGDG Checklist, and the GAR together with its HGDG PIMME Checklist including the other supporting reportorial contents; and (v) cause the optimal and judicious utilization of the GAD Funds to achieve the objective of providing equal development opportunities for men and women through gender-responsive PPAs.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The GAD Focal Person will prepare a GAD Agenda upon assumption of the new administration in July 2025. The CY 2024 GPB was submitted late; the GFPS committed to improve timeliness. The GFPS and Municipal Accountant will reconcile records. The GAD Focal Person and MPDO will complete HGDG-PIMME checklists and finalize CY 2025 GAD AR. The Municipal Mayor agreed to allocate adequate resources and ensure optimal utilization of GAD funds.
observations.reason: CY 2024 GPB/endorsement and GAD AR submissions were delayed; some attributions were not supported with HGDG-PIMME; GAD fund utilization was suboptimal (51.37% financial delivery rate).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant reconcile their respective records and make necessary adjustments to establish the correct balances of the RPT and SET Receivables accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The MTO and MAO reconciled the RPT/SET delinquencies records as of December 31, 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC ensure that the NOA and NTP for all procurement activities undertaken by the Municipality are posted in the PhilGEPS website, and attached to the contract a proof of posting in compliance with Sections 37.1.6 and 37.4.2 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: Implemented The NOA and NTP for all procurement undertakings in CY 2024 were posted in the PhilGEPS website.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Treasurer to (i) issue a check to transfer the CY 2021 unexpended/unutilized LDRRM Funds from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund amounting to ₱1,330,723.27; and (ii) with the help of the Municipal Accountant, to demand/remind the depository bank (PNB) to furnish them with Bank Statements and transfer the balances of the above accounts to either Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) or Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP), the AGDBs situated in the nearest locality, pursuant to Sections 5.2, 5.4 and 5.5 of the Department of Finance (DOF) Circular No. 001-2015 dated June 1, 2015, and Paragraph 1 of DOF Department Order No. 27-05 dated December 9, 2005, to ensure judicious management in a manner supportive of the development objectives of the government in accelerating economic growth and stability of government funds; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) prepare necessary entries to correct the misstatements in the accounts of Due to/from Other Funds, both in the books of General and Trust Funds and submit a copy of the corresponding JEV relative to the transfer of CY 2021 unexpended DRRM Funds amounting to ₱1,330,723.27 from the General Fund to the Trust Fund; (ii) submit the remaining SLs of the Cash in Bank accounts for the Audit Team to ensure the reliability of the balance reported, and henceforth, ensure complete submission of the Subsidiary Schedules/Ledgers to support the balances of the general ledger controlling account in compliance with COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010; and (iii) prepare the required Monthly BRS to reconcile the cash in bank balance per books and per bank and to ascertain the validity and accuracy of the Cash in Bank account as presented in the financial statements and submit the same to the Audit Team on or before the 10th day of the following month.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure proper classification of accounts in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 01, 2015, and be guided by the same Circular in recording transactions in the books of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
the BAC to explain the noted lapses and deficiencies in the conduct of the above-mentioned procurements, submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team, and henceforth, to strictly comply with the provisions outlined in the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;
the Municipal Accountant to (i) adhere strictly to the guidelines provided in the CSC-DBM Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2015 dated November 15, 2015, and CSC MC No. 18, s. 2020 dated October 15, 2020, in the processing of claims for overtime services; and (ii) submit all lacking/missing documents and the revised Summary of COVID- 19 Fund Sources and Utilization for CY 2021 to the Audit Team, and ensure that every voucher for the succeeding disbursements must be properly and completely supported with documentary requirements in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 to establish the validity and propriety of transactions; and
the MDRRMO to submit written justification for the non-observance of one-hour breaks for breakfast, lunch, or supper and rest, and every three hours of continuous overtime service, or as may be necessary in rendering overtime services pursuant to Item 3.3.2e of CSC MC No. 18, 2020 dated October 15, 2020.
With that, we recommended that for the succeeding periods, the Municipal Government to (a) require the employees to prepare a quantified accomplishment reports specific to the requested overtime services; and (b) ensure that all accomplishment reports of every employee are approved and signed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the BAC and its Technical Working Group (TWG) to immediately submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team and henceforth, ensure that all perfected contracts are properly and completely documented before submitting the same to the Audit Team and all procurement activities to be undertaken by the Municipality shall strictly be in accordance with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
the LDRRMO to incorporate the unimplemented PPAs in the succeeding year’s LDRRMFIP and ensure proper implementation thereof in accordance with its approved planned schedule of implementation, pursuant to Section 1, Rule 18 of the IRR of R.A. No. 10121; and
the Municipal Treasurer to issue a check to transfer the current year’s unexpended/unutilized LDRRM Funds from General Fund to the Special Trust Fund solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities within the next five years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor thru the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator:
properly evaluate, determine the expected output and properly plan the schedule of implementation of the unimplemented development PPAs in line with its Local Development Plan to achieve the desirable socio-economic development;
coordinate with other agency personnel to resolve the issues on the delay/non-implementation of identified development projects like preparation of Program of Works (POW) and lot negotiation to ensure that these projects could be feasibly undertaken, or if not, budget should be re-aligned to other eligible and viable developmental projects; and
coordinate with the BAC so that the identified PPAs in 20 percent DF will be incorporated in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP), so that the latter could schedule the procurement process to ensure that these PPAs are among the priority projects of the Municipality during the budget year.

We, therefore, recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
enjoin the GFPS and Municipal Budget Officer to allocate at least five percent of total annual appropriation to identified GAD-focused PPAs and properly integrate into Annual Budget/Annual Investment Program;
issue an appropriate policy directive to cause the full/complete implementation of the seven unimplemented PPAs and direct the GFPS to monitor them; and
henceforth, enjoin the GFPS to ensure that the attributions in the GPB are supported by HGDG Checklist and observe timely submission of the GAR and its HGDG PIMME Checklist pursuant to COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014.
However, we recommended that the Municipal Accounting Office to trace down the breakdown of unremitted balance of taxes withheld in prior years and effect adjusting entry/ies, if warranted.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor              a) require the BAC to explain then provide to the Audit Team the noted documentary deficiencies in the procurement process; and b) remind the Municipal Accountant to ensure henceforth the completeness of supporting documents prior to processing of vouchers for the payment to suppliers.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the LDRRMO (a) justify/explain the utilization of special trust fund without an updated or supplemental LDRRMFIP, supplemental AIP, and approved Supplemental Budget; and (b) henceforth ensure that the Special Trust Fund is being used only for DRRM PPAs that are specified in the approved plan or program.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor            (a) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign) to intensify the collection of real property tax and to persuade taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount; (b) require the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the year-end balances of RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred RPT and SET accounts.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor            (a) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare BRS and submit the same to the Audit Team on time; (b) require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to verify the status of stale check whether it is still on the hands of the payee and if necessary cancel and replace them, update the RCI, and prepare the journal entries in the books; (c) require the collectors/tellers to remit collections daily; and (d) provide security or police escort to the Municipal Treasurer and/or Disbursing Officer during their travel for bank transactions.

We hereby recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) create an Inventory Committee; (b) direct the Treasurer’s Office to complete the physical count that they have had started; and (c) ensure the preparation of RPCI and RPCPPE and the submission of which to the Audit Team on time.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to submit the above-enumerated lacking documents for the infrastructure projects listed in Annex G and the Municipal Accountant to impose the correct amount of liquidated damages against the contractors of the projects listed in Annexes H and I. Henceforth, ensure the completeness of supporting documents prior to processing of payments and the accuracy of the STE in the computation of the number of days of delay and amount of liquidated damages.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require (a) the contractor to repaint the noted benches; (b) the Municipal Engineer to provide the above-mentioned documents necessary to verify time extension, quality control and material testing; and (c) the Municipal Accountant impose the proper amount of liquidated damages for the above-mentioned projects with noted delays.

Therefore, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor a) require the Municipal Engineer to supervise closely the project implementation to ensure success of the project. Likewise, explain the delay in the project completion and apprise the Audit Team of its current status; and b) require the Municipal Accountant to deduct liquidated damages from any money due or which may become due the contractor for the updated number of days delayed in the project completion.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to update the Audit Team of the status of the eleven PPAs and fast track their implementation in order to maximize the utilization of 20% Development Fund.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) enjoin the GFPS to observe timely submission of the GPB and its HGDG Checklist and the GAR and its HGDG PIMME Checklist including the other supporting reportorial contents; and b) allow the GFPS to attend capacity-building or training on how to administer or accomplish the GAD-related tools such as but not limited to the HGDG Checklist and GMEF Questionnaire.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) enjoin the depository banks with which they maintain bank accounts to furnish them with bank statements regularly and promptly; (b) make sure that the request is communicated in writing for documentation and reference; (c) require the Municipal Accountant to prepare BRS, submit the same to the Audit Team on time, and reflect in the books all valid reconciling items; (d) require the Municipal Treasurer to monitor all checks and cancel any stale check and replace them with a new one if necessary; and (e) comply with DOF DC      No. 01-2017 by closing all bank accounts with the PNB and transfer them to the AGDBs within the province.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to adopt strategies in intensifying the collection of real property tax such as information campaign to encourage taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount; (b) require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their records and present the correct amount of tax delinquencies; and (c) require the Municipal Assessor to conduct a tax mapping to update the real property units and their corresponding assessed value.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) justify/explain the noted procurement of goods thru cash advance and ensure that the subsequent procurements are undertaken thru proper mode of procurement in compliance with R.A. No. 9184; (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to process only the request for cash advance of the officers and employees who have no outstanding balance; (c) require the concerned employees to refund immediately the DTE in excess of the allowable amount; and (d) improve the preventive controls over the liquidation of cash advance by issuing a written reminder or demand for the immediate settlement of outstanding balances.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: (a) the creation of an Inventory Committee for the conduct of complete physical count of inventories semestrally in compliance with Section 156 of  Circular No. 92-386 and Section 124 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and   (b) the Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee to reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete RPCI to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: (a) the Municipal Engineer to determine the useful life of the infrastructure assets and the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation allowance for the said accounts; (b) the Municipal Treasurer or the Inventory Committee to use the prescribed format of the RPCPPE and make sure to reconcile the same with the accounting records; and (c) cause the disposal of the unserviceable properties in compliance with the pertinent guidelines.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Assessor to process in the Registry of Deeds the TCT/CLT of the parcels of land of the municipality. Also, secure and submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents to support the disbursements made for the lot acquisition. Henceforth, all claims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation as required by Section 4.6 of P.D. No. 1445.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the AAO to make sure that the submission of ARA is made to the GSIS for the latter to update its MSP and Billing Statement. Also, remit to the GSIS on time in order to avoid payment of penalties.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to identify which unexpended balances above are pertaining to completed and fully paid projects and then return to the source agencies any unexpended funds from it. Secure Official Receipt for the same in compliance with Item Nos. 4.9 and 5.5 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994. Also, remind the supplier/contractor of their billing statements for the above projects which are not yet fully paid.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require: (a) the Municipal Engineer to submit the approved Bill of Quantities and Detailed Engineering Estimate; (b) the BAC to explain the deficiencies noted and to submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team and henceforth to adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of procurement process; and (c) the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of supporting documents prior to processing of claims for the infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Engineer to submit the lacking documents for the completed projects enumerated in Annex E and ensure strict compliance with Section 62 of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant that henceforth, each voucher must have complete supporting documents prior to processing of claims.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require (a) the contractor to repair the items found to be defective and install the items discovered to be missing; (b) the Municipal Engineer to provide the above-mentioned documents necessary to verify time extension, quality control and material testing and to determine the need to apply the warranty security should the contractor refuse to take remedial actions; and (c) the Municipal Accountant impose the proper amount of liquidated damages for the above-mentioned projects with noted delays.

With that, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the LDRRMO to ensure henceforth that the PPAs identified for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery be implemented and the funds therefore be utilized for the benefits of the target beneficiaries or constituents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer to justify/explain the non-implementation of three PPAs for the current appropriation and one PPA from continuing appropriation, and update the Audit Team of the status of the 24 ongoing PPAs; (b) fast track the implementation of identified developmental PPAs and maximize the utilization of the said fund; and (c) instruct the BAC to submit the copies of contracts and its bidding documents that form part of the contract for the nine ongoing projects and henceforth comply with COA Circular                  No. 2009-001 in the submission of contracts.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who also heads the Municipal Solid Waste Board, to (a) continuously follow up the approval of the NSWMC of the municipality’s 10-Year SWMP; (b) expedite the closure of open dump site and the development of Sanitary Landfill as final disposal site in compliance with Sections 17 and 37 of R.A. No. 9003;  (c) collaborate any information education and communication (IEC) campaigns with the barangay concerned and be more systematic in the identification of the specific IEC campaign activities to ensure its effectiveness; and (d) improve garbage collection by strictly enforcing waste segregation at source and at final disposal site.

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) issue an appropriate policy directive to implement the PPAs provided for in the GPB and that directive shall mandate the GFPS to monitor its implementation in compliance with Item 4.1.C of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and (b) require the GFPS to submit the HGDG Checklists on the PIMME in compliance with Item 7.3 of JMC No. 2016-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Treasurer to submit all monthly and quarterly financial and accounting reports and their corresponding statements within the prescribed period in order for the Audit Team to undertake timely review and post-audit of the transactions and accounts and at the same time to inform management of any defects or deficiencies.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that: (a) the
Municipal Accountant record in the books all valid reconciling items that
require adjustment and correction in the general ledger; and
(b) the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer draw the necessary adjusting
entry and correction to reflect the deposits made in trust fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and Treasurer to: (a) refrain from granting cash advance for the
procurement of goods in compliance with Item 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002
and 2016 IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 and justify the above procurement
transactions; and (b) strengthen the internal control on the liquidation of
cash advances by complying with the provisions under Item 5 of the same
COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the conduct of
complete physical count annually for PPE and semestrally for Inventories.
Also, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and
submit the complete RPCPPE and RPCI to the Audit Team pursuant to
Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) submit all valid
supporting documents for the recorded Accounts Payable totaling
P3,135,583.23 and henceforth ensure that only valid claims against
government funds are recorded as payable; (b) instruct the Municipal
Accountant to draw the necessary correcting entry for the Accounts Payable
amounting to P3,135,583.23 for having no supporting documents and
P162,664.38 for being the difference between the obligated amounts versus
actual expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant and Treasurer to return the unexpended fund totaling
P47,686.87 to its respective source agencies and secure Official Receipt for
the same in compliance with Item Nos. 4.9 and 5.5 of COA Circular
No. 94-013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to comply with DOF Department Order No. 27-05 and
Department Circular No. 001-2015 relative to a sound and efficient
management of government funds by transferring all accounts from private
bank to AGDB.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an appropriate
policy directive to implement the PPAs provided for in the GPB and the
directive shall mandate the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to monitor its
implementation in compliance with Item 4.1.C of the PCW-DILG-DBM-
NEDA JMC No. 2013-01. Moreover, the GFPS shall make an effort to
increase the utilization rate and the gender responsiveness of the PPAs being
charged and/or attributed to the GAD Fund in compliance with the same
JMC.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) instruct the
MDRRMO to fast track the implementation of the unexpended/unobligated
DRRM PPAs; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Budget Officer to revert to general fund for social services the special trust
fund pertaining to CY 2012 in compliance with Item No. 5.1.13 of COA
Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) require the BAC to
submit all lacking bid documents to the Audit Team and henceforth adhere
strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in all
stages of procurement process and ensure that all bid documents are
complete, evaluated and submitted on time; and (b) instruct the Municipal
Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of
supporting documents prior to processing of claims for payment of the above
infrastructure projects in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and
Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) thru the MPDC
ensure that the PPAs chargeable under the 20% Development Fund partake
the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the
attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management
outcomes in compliance with Item 3.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2017-1; and (b) fast track the implementation of identified
development PPAs and maximize the utilization of 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who also heads the
Municipal Solid Waste Board, to; (a) to follow up the approval of the
NSWMC to the Municipality’s 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan; (b)
ensure the closure of open dump sites and the development of Sanitary
Landfill as final disposal site for solid and residual wastes of the municipality
in compliance with Section Nos. 17 and 37 of R.A. 9003; (c) encourage active
participation of the barangays to support the SWM program of the
municipality by educating their respective constituents on the proper
segregation of wastes and the importance of their participation in the SWM
programs; and (d) through the MPDC, comply with the requirements of RA
No. 9003 on waste segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels on the
designated garbage collection area and ensure the establishment of
functional MRFs in all barangays.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count semestrally for Inventories and annually for PPE, reconcile the result with the records of the Municipal Accountant, and submit the duly certified and approved RPCI and RPCPPE to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The Auditor was informed that the Municipal Mayor has already advised/ordered the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee has not yet conducted physical count for PPE and Inventory.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to:
refrain from granting cash advance for the procurement of supplies and equipment in compliance with Item 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and justify the procurement transactions listed in Annex E; and
strengthen the internal control on the liquidation of cash advances by complying with the provisions under Item 5 of the same COA Circular.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct complete physical count annually for PPE and semestrally for Inventories. Also, reconcile the results of the count with the accounting records and submit the complete RPCPPE and RPCI to the Audit Team pursuant to Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to substantiate claims by verifying, analyzing, and validating the existence of accounts. Also, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to enforce collections, and if proven futile, comply with the guidelines and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 for the request for authority to write-off.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the disparity between the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from other funds and Due to other funds and present the correct balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
justify/explain the purchase and disbursement made to Isuzu Manila without undergoing proper mode of procurement and the ObR amount being lower than the cost of the vehicle;
require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit all lacking bid documents required by pertinent provisions of R.A. No. 9184 and other documentary requirements set forth in COA Circular                      No. 2012-001; and
make sure that the rescue vehicle has red license plate and marked with “for official use only”.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
instruct the Municipal Accountant to make sure that retention money is deducted and withheld from the total amount due even for the 100% first and final billing for the infrastructure projects and require the contractors to submit a substitute for the retention money in compliance with Item 6, Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A.             No. 9184;
direct the Municipal Engineer to submit the Statement of Time Elapsed, As-Built Plans for the projects and photo of tarpaulin signboard for the affected projects listed in Annex A in compliance with Item 9.1.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
require the Municipal Accountant to ensure henceforth that the vouchers for the progress and final billings for the infrastructure projects are supported with complete documentation to avoid issuance of suspension and/or disallowance in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor who also heads the Municipal Solid Waste Board to follow up the comment and/or approval of the NSWMC to the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan transmitted by the municipality. Moreover, ensure the closure of open dump sites and the development of Sanitary Landfill as final disposal site for solid and residual wastes of the municipality in compliance with Section Nos. 17 and 37 of R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that: 

 monthly BRS are properly prepared and submitted for a timely verification of the accounts;

 Cash in Bank accounts are properly and regularly reconciled with the Municipal Treasurer’s records; and

 necessary adjustments/ reconciling items including stale checks are immediately effected in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted the BRS for CY 2017 and made the necessary adjustments for the reconciling items.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: 

 Enforce the immediate liquidation/ settlement of long outstanding cash advances;  and

 Instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strengthen the internal control by complying with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in granting cash advance and the Revised IRR of Republic Act No. 9184 in resorting to alternative mode of procurement when there are failed biddings.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant exerted effort to locate the address of the accountable officers concerned.
observations.reason: The unliquidated cash advance has a balance of P434,470.40 as of December 31, 2017. It was observed that in CY 2017, the Municipality still granted cash advance for the procurement activities of the municipality. Further, liquidation was also done belatedly by the accountable officers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the disparity between the balances of the reciprocal accounts Due from other funds and Due to other funds and present the correct balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant exerted effort to trace and reconcile the noted discrepancy. 

Decrease in discrepancy was observed from P2,424,504.90 in CY 2016 to only P981,966.27 in CY 2017.
observations.reason: There was still discrepancy of P981,966.27 between the reciprocal accounts in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and the Accountant to remit the excess and unutilized PDAF to the Bureau of Treasury/DOF and ask Official Receipt for the same in compliance with Sections 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated        December 13, 1994 and pursuant to Supreme Court pronouncements declaring PDAF unconstitutional.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Accountant have already remitted the remaining PDAF balances from completed projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to adhere strictly to the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in all stages of the procurement process. Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to scrutinize every voucher for the completeness and veracity of supporting documents prior to processing of claims for payment in compliance with Section 4.6 of P.D. No. 1445 and Item 9 of COA Circular     No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has provided the Audit Team with copies of lacking Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF).
observations.reason:
Other lacking supporting documents were not yet provided. 

Also, based on the submitted subsidiary ledger for CY 2017, no adjustment was made for account Water Supply System for the completed project erroneously recorded as an expense.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Treasurer to submit all monthly and quarterly financial and accounting reports and their corresponding statements within the prescribed period in order for the Audit Team to undertake timely review and post-audit of the transactions and accounts and at the same time to inform management of any defects and/or deficiencies.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant has submitted all financial statements and reports to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the formulation of an Annual Work and Financial Plan and the allocation of 1% of IRA for the PPAs of the LCPC. In addition, make sure the preparation and submission of the documents required under Item II of the DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Government attributed the amount of P1,083,000.00 for LCPC per review of the Sanggunian Panlalawigan per Resolution No. 3-2017-51.
observations.reason: Some of the reports enumerated in page 40 of the AAR 2016 was not yet submitted to the Audit Team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Fast track the implementation of identified developmental PPAs and maximize the utilization of 20% Development Fund; and

 Ensure compliance with the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 on the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund.
observations.managementAction:
Out of 28 PPAs noted to be not unimplemented as of December 31, 2016, only six remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2016.

Also, the Municipal Budget Officer has appropriate the correct amount for 20% Development Fund for CY 2017.
observations.reason:
The Audit Team still observed unimplemented projects under 20% Development Fund. 

Also, there were projects charged against the said fund which were inconsistent or unrelated with Item 3 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

 cause the proper implementation of the unexpended/ unobligated DRRM programs/activities/projects; and

 require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to adhere strictly with the requirements in Item 5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality has purchased rescue vehicle in CY 2017, one of the projects noted as unimplemented in CY 2016.

Moreover, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant have transferred the unexpended balance of LDRRMF (QRF and MOOE) to special trust fund on September 2017.
observations.reason: Other than the procurement of rescue vehicle, life jackets and life rings, the remaining noted unimplemented PPAs as of December 31, 2016 remained unimplemented as of December 31, 2017.

See to it that balances of reciprocal accounts are in balance at all times; and Adhere to the provisions of NGAS Manual in the use of reciprocal accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: That respective annual/semestral physical count of Property, Plant, and Equipment and Inventories and the required RPCPPE and RPCI, respectively, be given top priority and submit said reports to the concerned audit team on the dates required by above COA rules; The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their respective records with the RPCPPE and RPCI prepared by the inventory committee and effect the necessary adjustments to finally reflect existence and true value the PPE and inventory accounts; and The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to always maintain stock/property cards and supplies/property ledger cards, respectively.
observations.managementAction: An inventory committee was created and physical count was already conducted for CY 2016. However, the RPCPPE and RPCI was not submitted to the Audit team.
observations.reason: Management experienced difficulties in the conduct of physical count of PPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Instruct the accountable official/s to complete all the necessary documents needed in the submission of the Request for Relief from Property Accountability with the Commission on Audit, including the Inventory List of the Property, Plant and Equipment amounting to P6,088,747.05, lost through fire on August 23, 2010; and Upon approval of said Request for Relief from Property Accountability, instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw a JEV, supported with complete necessary documents, to effect adjustments.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for all accounts (active or non-moving) of the agency in order to be assured that the balances of all LCCAs (per books and per banks) are in or brought to agreement; and The Municipal Treasurer, as account signatories, reiterate in writing, duly received by the depository bank/s, that copies of bank statements, for all accounts of the agency (active and non-moving), be sent to the agency every end of each month for the preparation of the Bank Reconciliation Statement.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant said that they will try their best to prepare BRS for all accounts on time upon receipt of bank statement from depository banks.
observations.reason: Delay in BRS preparation due to the delay in the receipt of bank statement from the depository banks.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant request from the office of the Municipal Mayor, copies of transmittal letters of the Liquidation Reports of Intelligence Expenses amounting to P405,000.00, sent to the Commission Proper, and furnish the same to the audit team.
observations.reason: Non submission of liquidation report by the concerned Mayor on intelligence cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: Coordinate with LGU offices affecting the timely submission of accounts, monthly, quarterly financial reports/statements/schedules within the reglementary period as required under the afore-cited COA rules and regulation; and Seriously adhere to the provisions of the above-cited COA rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant said that they will try to submit their accounts and financial reports on time or lessen the number of days of delay.
observations.reason: Difficulty of travel due to geographical location and unavailability of personnel to go to travel contributed to the delay of submission of accounts and financial reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that both the provisions of the Revised Administrative Code of the Philippines and Section 04(s) of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1, are adhered to in the recognition and set-up of liability accounts at year-end.
observations.managementAction: All liability accounts that were set up at year-end were supported only with OBRs.
observations.reason: All liability accounts which have been obligated are supported by documents but were not attached to the JEV but in the voucher so there will be supporting documents at the time of payment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Instruct the concerned municipal officials to remit all unused DAP/PDAF funds to the Bureau of Treasury/DOF and ask Official Receipt for the same in compliance to Sections 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December 13, 1994 and pursuant to Supreme Court pronouncements declaring PDAF and some DAP acts and practices unconstitutional. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to evaluate the need for the unexpended balances; Request for approval from the source agency for the use of said funds, if still needed for other projects or purposes of the municipality, and Instruct for the remittance to the National Treasury as required under Executive Order No. 431 dated May 30, 2005, in case said funds proved to be no longer needed.
observations.managementAction: There was already partial refund of these funds for CY 2016.
observations.reason: There are still balances which are not yet returned to the source agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: Look into the non-moving or dormant portion of the Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts and initiate reconciliation with the concerned agencies for any discrepancy/ies noted; and Effect remittance of the correct amounts due said agencies in order to avoid or lessen any applicable interests and/or penalties, which might accrue thereon.
observations.managementAction: It has already been properly accounted for.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management furnish COA with the Sangguniang-Bayan Resolution supporting the grant of cell card allowances to the agency personnel.
observations.managementAction: To furnish COA Office with the Sangguniang-Bayan Resolution pertaining to the Mobile allowance of municipal officials and employees.
observations.reason: The Sanggunian has been informed of the matter and they promised to comply/furnish the Audit a copy of resolution. For the meantime, internal control in disbursing process is being observed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: Require the BAC to submit all lacking documents; Direct the BAC to post all procurement opportunities stated in the Invitation to Bid for infrastructure projects in conspicuous places in the premises of the agency within seven (7) calendar days as certified by the head of the BAC Secretariat; Instruct the BAC to observe strictly the provisions of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; and Instruct the BAC to avoid recurrence of the noted observations.
observations.managementAction: To strictly adhere to all COA recommendations.
observations.reason: There was a print out of PhilGEPS posting but was not certified by the BAC. Issuance of Notice of Award was delayed due to delay in posting of Performance Bond of the Contractors. Poor internet connection is the main reason in COA Finding No. 10, Item g. There was delay in the perfection of the Contract and Agreement as cited in Finding No. 10, Item I for the reason that the Contractor is not from the Municipality of Cajidiocan, therefore it will take time before it will be sent back to BAC Secretariat/Chairman.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC Chairman ensure the timely submission of copies of government contracts/purchase orders together with each of all the documents forming parts thereof within five (5) working days, from execution/issuance, to warrant timely review and evaluation of the same and enable the audit team to promptly inform the management of the deficiencies, if any. We further recommended that the BAC Chairman ensure that all entries in the Purchase Orders should be completely and properly filled up. We finally recommended that the BAC adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 16, 1996.
observations.managementAction: To accomplish the recommendations of COA.
observations.reason: Distance of Agency to the COA Office and difficulty of travel contributed to the delay of submission.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensures that no less than five percent (5%) of the total budget for GAD appropriation. Moreover, ensure that the municipal officials concerned develop and submit a gender-responsive GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) which is reviewed and duly approved by the DILG Regional Office and incorporated to the agency’s Annual Budget, in compliance to Joint Circular No. 2001-01 of the DILG, DBM and NCRFW and PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: To follow strictly the GAD Guidelines/mandates

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that: Not less than five per cent (5%) of the estimated revenue from regular sources shall be set aside as the LDRRMF to support disaster risk management activities; The Municipal Accountant see to it that the disbursements funded out of the LDRRM Fund are properly supported with required documentation; The Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (LDRRMO) submit on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council (LDC) to the COA auditor, the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF, prepared and certified correct by the Municipal Accountant.; and All other provisions cited under COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 are adhered to/implemented.
observations.reason: The PPAs under the LDRMM Plan was not fully implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: always ensure: Full implementation/utilization of the 20% minimum appropriation for Development Fund; and Strict adherence to the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 on the utilization of the 20% Development Fund.
observations.reason: Prior year’s unimplemented projects were already started. However, there were still unimplemented projects for the CY 2016 developmental projects.
